answer:
In Iqbal's "Husn o Ishq," beauty (*husn*) and love (*ishq*) form a dynamic interdependence, where each perfects and manifests the other: beauty spurs love toward its fulfillment, while love gives beauty its vital expression.[^1] Ghalib, by contrast, renders their relation aporetic—beauty is indifferent and self-perpetuating, requiring no lover for its display, while prolonged attachment to its sensory illusions merits transcendence.[^2]

**Iqbal:**  
> You are the lightning-flash of Beauty; I, Love's culmination,  
> If You are dawn, my tears become Your dew's creation.[^3]  
>   
> From Beauty, Love's own nature is spurred toward perfection;  
> From You, the saplings of my hopes find green direction.[^4]

Here, love (*ishq*) reaches its apex through beauty's radiant agency, even supplying its own complementary elements (tears as dew); beauty, in turn, directs and vitalizes love's growth.

**Ghalib:**  
> Indifferent Beauty is the one to pay for a new display;  
> The mirror, lost in thought, invents the way for a new display.[^5]  
>   
> O Mind, how long be lost in all of color's play?  
> The truly opened eye must turn away from a new display.[^6]

Beauty (*husn-e-beparwa*, "indifferent beauty") funds its own endless renewal via the lover's reflective gaze (the "mirror"), but this is mere illusion ("color's play"); true insight demands detachment, leaving love's dependence unresolved and futile.

Iqbal resolves the relation teleologically—beauty and love co-evolve toward perfection—while Ghalib leaves it suspended in irony: beauty thrives without love, which risks self-deception unless renounced.[^7]

[^1]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 13 ("Husn o Ishq"), [translation/](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/13-husn-o-ishq/translation/).

[^2]: *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 79 ("Husn-e-beparwa kharidar-e-mata-e-jalwa hai"), [translation/](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/79-husn-e-beparwa-kharidar-e-mata-e-jalwa-hai/translation/).

[^3]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 13, [p-73eab8](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/13-husn-o-ishq/translation/#p-73eab8).

[^4]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 13, [p-7a49e3](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/13-husn-o-ishq/translation/#p-7a49e3).

[^5]: *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 79, [p-6fb822](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/79-husn-e-beparwa-kharidar-e-mata-e-jalwa-hai/translation/#p-6fb822).

[^6]: *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 79, [p-bfa438](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/79-husn-e-beparwa-kharidar-e-mata-e-jalwa-hai/translation/#p-bfa438).

[^7]: The works share cosine similarity (0.36 corpus-wide), underscoring thematic proximity amid philosophical divergence. *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, [work wiki](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/wiki/_work.card.md); *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [work wiki](https://falsafa.corpus/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/wiki/_work.card.md).
